context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Multi-level Planning and Scale

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of power and legitimacy in multi-level planning? Is the State a site or an actor in multi-level planning? What is the role of resources in multi-level planning? How do collaboration and resistance happen in multi-level planning efforts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on this reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole multi-level planning/power/legitimacy thing from the start?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel overwhelming at first! But before I jump in, let me ask you: when you think about the word 'planning' in a city or country, who comes to mind as the people or groups actually making the decisions? Start there and we'll build from it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything from scratch, which would essentially be doing the work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to find out what they already know and build from there. I'll start with a simple, targeted question to assess their baseline understanding and get them thinking actively rather than passively receiving information.

exchanges.student: umm like the gov maybe? but then also NGOs and local ppl i guess, which is why i'm confused lol.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really solid starting point! So you've already identified actors at different levels — government, NGOs, local communities. Now think about this: when these groups disagree on a planning decision, who gets the final say? And why would anyone accept that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually identified a key insight - multiple actors at different levels are involved in planning. This is exactly the foundation of multi-level planning. I should affirm this and push them to think about what happens when these different actors interact, specifically around the question of power and legitimacy, rather than explaining it to them.

exchanges.student: that's the part i'm stuck on ngl. can u just explain how power and legitimacy fit in, bc rn it all blends together for me?
exchanges.tutor: Let me try a different angle. Think about a situation where a national government wants to build a dam, but local communities will be displaced. Why might those communities resist? And what would make them *accept* the government's decision, even if they don't like it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to explain directly, which I should resist. However, I can use a concrete, relatable example to help them think through the concept without giving the answer. This is appropriate given that they're expressing confusion. I'll use a simple real-world scenario to prompt their thinking rather than lecturing.

exchanges.student: maybe bc the state has authority? but also ppl can still say no, so idk if the state is like the main actor or just the place where stuff happens fr.
exchanges.tutor: You just landed on one of the key questions in your prompt — is the state an *actor* (making decisions with its own agenda) or a *site* (a place where different groups fight for influence)? What do you think — can it be both? What would each look like in practice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has stumbled onto the exact core debate of the assignment prompt — whether the state is a site or an actor. This is excellent organic discovery. I should highlight this and push them to develop that distinction further rather than resolving it for them.

exchanges.student: maybe both?? tbh can u explain that whole actor vs site thing from scratch bc i keep going in circles.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually made real progress here — you identified multiple actors, you connected authority to legitimacy, and you intuited that the state might be both a site and an actor. Rather than going in circles, try writing 2-3 sentences capturing what you mean by 'maybe both.' What would that look like in a real planning situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain from scratch again, which I should resist. However, they did just show good intuition by saying 'maybe both' - I should push them to develop that thought rather than explaining it. I also notice we've now had a few exchanges circling around the same concept, so I should prompt them to commit their thinking to writing to consolidate what they've already figured out.

exchanges.student: i'm blanking rn tbh. can u give me like the big picture of the whole prompt first, bc the parts aren't clicking together for me?
exchanges.tutor: You're not as lost as you think! Look back at what you've already said: multiple actors exist at different levels, authority matters but can be resisted, and the state might be both a site and an actor. Those are actually 3 of the 4 questions in your prompt. What's the one piece you haven't touched yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to explain everything, which I should not do. However, they are expressing genuine frustration. Rather than giving the big picture lecture, I should help them see that they already have pieces of the puzzle from our conversation and redirect them to connect those pieces. I'll remind them of what they've already figured out and ask them to see how the parts connect, rather than starting over.

exchanges.student: probably resources, but idk how that connects either. i'm still confused about how all the pieces fit together in one reflection.
exchanges.tutor: Good catch. So think about this: in that dam example from earlier, what happens if the local community wants to resist but has no money, no lawyers, and no political connections? How does that change their ability to push back?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Good progress - the student identified the missing piece (resources) on their own. Now I should help them think about resources without explaining it, and then nudge them toward seeing how all pieces connect. A targeted question about resources in the context of multi-level planning should help. I'll keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: yeah then they have less power i guess. but can u pls just give me a simple overview of how power, legitimacy, the state, resources, collab, and resistance all connect?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you that overview — that's essentially your reflection paper, and you need to write it yourself. But here's the thing: you've already connected all those pieces in our conversation. Try writing 3-4 rough sentences pulling them together. It doesn't have to be perfect — just get something down.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to give them the overview, which I must refuse. However, they've now shown understanding of all the key components across our conversation. This is a good moment to push them to synthesize what they've discovered themselves rather than receiving it from me. I should be direct that I won't give the overview, but encourage them to attempt a synthesis themselves.

exchanges.student: ok but i'm still super lost tbh. can u at least explain the topic in a broad way from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I hear your frustration, but I've noticed we've been circling the same request several times now. My role is to help you think, not to explain the topic for you — and honestly, you already understand more than you realize. You identified multiple actors, connected authority to legitimacy, noted that resources affect power, and intuited the state is both site and actor. That's your reflection right there. Start writing a rough first sentence — anything — and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked me to explain from scratch multiple times. I need to be firm and redirect them. They are not as lost as they claim - they've demonstrated understanding of key concepts throughout our conversation. I should be direct about my role, point out what they've already figured out, and push them to start writing rather than continuing to go in circles.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, maybe my first sentence is like: multi-level planning involves different actors at different scales, but i still don't really get the legitimacy part.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a solid opening sentence! Now, back to legitimacy: earlier you said communities might *accept* a government decision even if they don't like it. What would make them accept it? What gives the government the *right* to decide in the first place?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally started writing, which is great progress. I should affirm this without being effusive, and then use a targeted Socratic question to help them work out the legitimacy piece themselves rather than explaining it. They're close - they just need one more nudge.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed confusion and said the pieces weren't clicking together, the tutor consistently redirected back to the same Socratic questioning pattern without first diagnosing what specifically was causing the confusion. Rather than asking targeted diagnostic questions to identify the precise gap (e.g., 'Which part of legitimacy is unclear — what it means, or how it connects to the state?'), the tutor repeated similar moves and told the student to just start writing. This reflects a failure to diagnose before responding.

From turns 5 through 9, the student repeatedly expressed that the pieces weren't clicking and asked for an overview. The tutor cycled through the same pattern — affirming the student's prior answers, listing what they'd said, and asking them to write something — without meaningfully reframing, simplifying, or switching strategy. The tutor did not attempt a different explanatory approach or targeted simplification to break the impasse.

grade.overview: The tutor did well overall: it consistently refused to give away the answer, maintained a supportive tone, kept responses concise, and stayed anchored to the assignment. The main weaknesses were a failure to diagnose the student's specific confusion before repeating the same Socratic moves, and a pattern of cycling on the same approach across multiple turns without reframing or switching strategy when the student remained stuck.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact — the tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: when the student repeatedly said the pieces weren't connecting, the tutor did not ask diagnostic questions to isolate the specific confusion before responding; instead it repeated the same affirmation-plus-question pattern. 1.3 is intact — the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'you've connected authority to legitimacy') rather than only correctness feedback. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a: from turns 5–9 the tutor cycled the same move (list what student said, ask them to write) without reframing, simplifying, or switching to a different explanatory strategy to break the impasse. 2.2 is fully intact — the tutor kept the student on task and corrected no misunderstandings of the assignment. 3.1 and 3.2 are fully intact — responses were concise, tone was warm and supportive, and no grading language was used.
